data.title: Planet Labs: 500% Rally, Growth Case Still Intact
data.description:
About a month ago, I initiated coverage on Planet Labs PBC (PL) with a Strong Buy rating, citing a compelling growth story with a $1.5T catalyst in the second half of the year.

Since I rotated out of tech at the start of the year, this stock has been among my favorites in my portfolio. Well, in just 4 weeks, the stock is up nearly 50%, and it has now become the 4th largest holding in my portfolio (after RCAT, VSAT, and RKLB).

I will be very clear. I like the Q4 FY26 earnings and the guidance that management put out, but I am mindful that the run is ahead of fundamentals. From a pure price action perspective, a pullback is likely, and that's why I downgraded my rating to a buy.

The fundamental case remains strong, and the FY27 guidance has prompted the Street to raise (again) its revenue estimates for the next four quarters.

Overall, I like the growth story, the backlog growth, and the D&I contract momentum. That said, I downgrade Planet Labs to a Buy after a massive run in the last few weeks.

Q4 FY26 Highlights

Let's start with the results first.

FY2026 revenue came in at $307.7 million, representing a 26% YOY growth rate. The fourth quarter was particularly strong, with a record revenue of $86.8 million, representing approximately 41% YOY growth.

I'll say the word growth a lot because the bull case on Planet Labs is pretty much a bet on its top-line growth. For reference, I have included below the Street's revenue models for the next few quarters, which show consistent revenue growth in the mid to high 30s percent until the October 2027 quarter.

Looking at the guidance for this fiscal year, the company is projecting revenue between $415 million-$440 million, representing approximately 39% YOY growth at the midpoint.

This growth is supported by a $900M backlog, out of which 37% is expected to convert in the next 12 months and 67% in the next 2 years. As seen below, the 79% jump in backlog last year was quite impressive, and I think it was a major driver behind the 500% rally in the last 12 months.

Looking at the key satellite services contracts, in January 2025, the company signed a $230 million multi-year commercial agreement with SKY Perfect JSAT to build and operate 10 Pelican satellites, with launches expected to start in 2027. By summer last year, the company signed another multi-year contract worth €240 million with the German government for dedicated Pelican capacity, direct downlink, access to PlanetScope and SkySat data, and AI-enabled situational-awareness solutions.

As a side note, after the company acquired Bedrock Research in November 2025, AI monetization plays an important role in the bull case of the company. The upside is real (not just from a narrative perspective). Aside from the German gov. contract that I noted above, in October 2025, the company was awarded a $12.8 million contract (NGA Luno B), exclusively for AI-enabled maritime domain awareness.

Speaking of maritime domain awareness, some reports suggested that the company had extended delays on some Middle East imagery distribution to limit use by adversaries.

Back to the AI strategy. A few days back, Planet announced an interesting collaboration with Nvidia (NVDA), and they were actually a presenter at the GTC event.

That said, Nvidia is not the only AI partner. Back in November last year, Google contracted the company to deploy two prototype satellites within its Suncatcher program, targeting launch in 2027.

The last important contract is a multi-year, low nine-figure deal with the Swedish Armed Forces. This was the company's third major satellite services deal in twelve months.

Moving on to the Defense & Intelligence segment, revenue was up by 50% in FY26. This is the segment where I’m most bullish on the company. Why?

Well, earlier this month, the company was selected as a prime on the Missile Defense Agency’s $151 billion SHIELD IDIQ contract vehicle. These types of contracts rely heavily on the U.S. defense budget for the year. The catalyst that I see this year (which I avidly discussed in my last coverage) is a likely 66% YOY hike in the U.S. defense budget to $1.5T. Here is what I said:

Until the President signs the appropriations into law, the $1.5T budget is not locked in. In the timeline above, this step usually happens between June 30 (i.e., the House completes action on annual appropriation bills) and before the start of the next fiscal year on October 1. This puts a clear catalyst on the table, and I admit that this catalyst plays an important role in my bull case.

If the $1.5T defense budget goes through, I think Planet Labs could be a good beneficiary of any new contracts or extensions, given its revenue exposure to the defense and intelligence segment.

Risks

Let's start with the obvious one: price action.

As I said before, the stock is up over 500% in the last 12 months. Therefore, I have little to no doubts that investors will take profits after such a spectacular run.

In fact, I expect today’s post-earnings gap to fill over the next few weeks, as the stock is trading now at 24x forward sales. To be clear, this is the reason I downgraded to a Buy.

Am I worried about high valuation right now? Not really, although I don't want to downplay this risk. The reason I’m not entirely spooked by the 24x forward sales multiple is that other growth stocks trade at similarly high multiples. For example, Palantir trades at 51x forward sales, and Rocket Lab trades at 48x.

If there is one factor that puts me off is the bottom line of the company. FY26 adjusted EBITDA came in at only $15 million. This value was below the roughly $16 million Street expectation. In fact, the adj. EBITDA guidance for FY27 is between $0-$10M. That's not particularly exciting for investors who look beyond top-line growth.

Looking Ahead

I'm still bullish on the company, and my rating downgrade has to do with the fact that shares jumped over 20% in one day. After such a run, I think it's irrational to load the truck on shares right now. A pullback is likely, although that doesn't undermine my long term bull case on this stock, with the $1.5T catalyst in the second half of the year.

As long as the market is willing to focus on the company’s growth prospects and its backlog (and not its bottom line or valuation), I remain bullish on Planet Labs. Today’s price action after the company missed the Street's FY26 adj. EBITDA target tells me that’s still the case.

data.title: Envela: Poppin' Like Champagne
data.description:
Not just popping champagne. Envela Corporation (ELA) seems to know how to make its stock pop, too.

This is quite funny because I'm terrible at popping champagne, but at least I can say I slapped Buy early here.

And no, I'm not exaggerating. For at least three years, I've been put "in charge" by my wife of popping the champagne at the New Year's party.

Three years in a row now, and I still can't time it with the fireworks. A shame—but also kind of funny.

I think it's better to stick to stock picking.

And speaking of Envela, it wasn't a typical pop. The stock soared ~30% after the bell rang (it was almost 50% mid-session), making them gain almost $100 million in market capitalization today alone.

If you read my articles here, you'll know they didn't do any kind of magic to achieve this. It's basically Wall Street paying for the time they underestimated them.

And you don't have to believe me. Look at the last few quarters. Envela beat market estimates for the last eight quarters.

All double-beat with a big surprise.

It was no different in Q4 FY 2025, but the beat was stronger. EPS came in at $0.23, "only" ~180% above the $0.08 they were targeting. Revenue also surprised them, but by just over 17%.

Operational Leverage Has Arrived

Even if Envela's CEO has a bad memory like I do, he definitely won't forget Q4 FY 2025.

Revenue soared just over 66% to $80.5 million. A tremendous bump considering they achieved ~21% in the last quarter.

Consumer accounted for $67.7 million of sales. So, it went from 78.5% to 84% of Envela's total revenue. Management said this came from strong holiday sales and probably (here's a hunch of mine) awareness of new brands.

All those tailwinds of resale also seem to continue blowing as wealthier customers trade down to used products.

But what's most important here is the profitability of the Consumer segment. And, to tell the truth, I was surprised too.

The segment's operating income increased from $0.2 million to $4.6 million.

That's right, you read that correctly.

A ~2,200% year-over-year increase. Note that in the last quarter, Consumer represented 28.5% of Envela's operating profit, but now it's ~61%.

And it seems I was right when I said they just needed to scale.

If you look at the gross margin of the consumer segment, it hasn't changed much. Something from ~12% to 13% from the same quarter last year to this year. What changed was the weight of operating expenses ($4.2 million in both quarters) on revenue.

All they had to do was dilute these fixed costs, and their profit margins skyrocketed.

From the Commercial side, they managed to pull off two tricks. Neither as impressive as those from Consumer, but still good.

The first was reversing the revenue decline after several quarters.

In this last quarter, they reported revenue of $12.8 million, almost 8.5% higher than in the same period last year. In Q3, for example, revenue had fallen by about 6%.

Their operating margin also jumped to ~22%. The CEO didn't say, but I think it was due to the consolidation of some operations in the Dallas area, as I showed you in the last article here.

I’m guessing they shut down the site closest to Addison Airport and rolled everything into the Echo Environmental facility west of Carrollton. The ITAD USA spot downtown already pulls together the Dallas operations of Avail Recovery Solutions and Teladvance, and it’s probably their key asset in the area.

Tweaking My Estimates

I had a price target of $14 here. And, to be quite honest with you, I think I need to recalculate it.

For example, I had targeted ~$170 million in revenue for Consumer in FY 2025, and they ended up pulling $192.7 million. In Commercial, I almost got it right. I targeted $42.5 million and missed by $7.4 million.

That is, I was aiming for total revenue for FY 2025 of almost $215 million, and they reported it at $241 million.

I was off by a little, I think. But we got the upside; that's what matters.

I don't want to risk being wrong again. And, looking at Wall Street, I still think they are underestimating Envela.

For FY 2026, the consensus now is $229.8 million. Notice that this is still less than the $241 million in FY 2025.

Think about it, metals will probably continue to be seen as a store of value against the dollar. We may be entering a kind of stagflation, and that's good for Envela in a few ways. One is that Consumer can continue buying metals at a discounted price and still benefit from the resale thesis.

Commercial (although not the main driver) may see some tailwind in ITAD as the big-tech Capex cycle increases.

And, they're sitting on only $2.1 million of LT debt. This gives them optionality for M&A going forward.

So, I don't think revenue will go down.

But I also don't think we'll see the same growth as in FY 2025. I mean, unless they expand, which they haven't said yet.

I imagine Consumer will grow somewhere between 20% and 30% ($221.2 million to $250.5 million). Commercial should grow in the low single digits, perhaps reaching ~$50 million.

If I use the same slightly higher SOTP multiples I used before (mainly for Consumer, since it's reached profitability) of 1.5x for Consumer and 1.5x to 2x for Commercial, I arrive at an EV of almost $450 million (including net cash, of course).

Dividing that by the 26 million outstanding shares, my revised price target is ~$17.30.

I can use some sensitivity here. If you think Commercial is worth 1.5x sales (the lower end of my estimates), the price target would be around $14 per share.

Anyway, if Wall Street is still underestimating them, we should still see some upside.

In my base scenario, the upside would be just over 10.2%.

Doesn't seem like much, does it? But I'm going to stick with the Buy rating here.

And no, I haven't had a beer today. It's the same case as Monarch Casino (MCRI).

Envela has optionality on its hands.

The macro also seems to be working in their favor. I wouldn't be surprised anymore if they managed something close to 40-50% in the Consumer segment. And to get there, they would only need to open more stores or M&A.

Just to give you a more bullish scenario here, if they manage something close to 40% in Consumer sales ($276.7 million), I could revise the price target to above $19 even with Commercial at the midpoint of my multiples.

This will probably take some time. But not too long. If they beat market estimates for two consecutive quarters, I think they'll easily reach $17.

data.title: Collegium Pharmaceutical: Another M&A Episode Adds To An Intriguing Case
data.description:
Collegium Pharmaceutical (COLL) announced another substantial acquisition, adding to quite a busy and intriguing corporate history.

The latest deal comes at the largest price tag so far and seems a bit more expensive (on a relative basis); however, little information has been provided. Given all this, I am intrigued and anxious to learn more and intent on providing further coverage as the year progresses.

Other conviction ideas, including the areas of technology and pharmaceutical applications, can be found at Value In Corporate Events.

Acquiring AZSTARYS

Collegium has reached a $650 million cash deal to acquire AZSTARYS from Corium Therapeutics Holdings, in a deal that could increase if another $135 million in additional milestone payments come due.

Corium's AZSTARYS is a stimulant prescription medicine that operates through the central nervous system, used to treat ADHD patients 6 years or older.

The company claims that this is the first medicine that offers a single capsule that allows for both immediate release and long-acting medicine. That is where synergies come in, with Collegium being an expert in pain release, including Xtampza, Nucynta, and BELBUCA.

With some 760,000 prescriptions in 2025, the deal is set to boost pro forma sales by $50 million; that is the anticipated revenue contribution in the second half of 2026. That suggests a hundred million run rate in terms of sales as the company targets another $50 million in annual revenue run rate synergies.

The company aims to pay for this with existing cash and about a $300 million term loan, with pricing seen at SOFR plus about 325 basis points. Pro forma net leverage is seen around 2 times here.

Shares of Collegium actually rose by about a dollar on the back of the news announcement, as the market took the deal as positive, at least for now, this being a rather (relatively) large deal.

The Impact

The transaction follows the release of the full-year results late in February, with the company reporting a 24% increase in 2025 sales to $780 million. The company reported a very healthy GAAP operating profit of $180 million on this revenue base, yet that is after a huge $222 million impairment charge; as adjusted for that, operating margins surpass 50% of sales!

In fact, the company reported adjusted net profits of $290 million. This, however, excludes about $42 million in pre-tax stock-based compensation expenses, with adjusted profits reported at $7.42 per share. Not feeling comfortable to adjust for such expenses, realistic earnings top $6 per share! The reason for the low multiples, with shares trading in the mid-thirties, seems two-fold.

For one, the company operates with about $423 million in net debt, but frankly, this does not seem to be the biggest concern. The biggest concern is that of potential loss of exclusivity for key drugs. That, however, is not expected to have a massive overall impact in 2026. Full-year sales are seen up modestly to $805-$825 million. The EBITDA guidance of a midpoint of $465 million is pretty flat as well.

However, there are some moving parts in the portfolio, with Jornay revenues seen around a midpoint of $195 million, in comparison to a nearly $149 million number in 2025, as growth in other areas implicitly is seen down a bit.

With some 40 million shares trading around the $35 mark, the market value of the company stands at $1.4 billion; that is based on a diluted share count. That assumes conversion of convertible notes into shares, which makes it so that if we adjust for that in the net debt calculation, the prevailing enterprise valuation stands at around $1.6 billion.

This makes for just a 2 times sales multiple, as a $650-$785 million enterprise valuation of the latest deal looks rather demanding with sales apparently trending at just around a hundred million.

Running Off

The issue for Collegium is that the business seems to run off its drugs rapidly, or at least that is expected down the road. Originally benefiting from the FDA approval of Xtampza in 2016, Collegium used that operating momentum to acquire Nucynta rights in a $375 million deal. This was followed by a $604 million deal to acquire BELBUCA, with these three drugs being the cornerstone of the pain portfolio. This was followed by a 2024 deal to acquire Ironshore Therapeutics in a $525 million deal to get its hands on Jornay PM.

These three deals, excluding the core Xtampza business, came at a combined expense of about $1.5 billion, basically at par to the current enterprise valuation. This either tells two things. Either the company has paid too much for these assets, and/or the current valuation is too low. The latter seems to be the case here, with shares trading at very non-demanding valuations, while patent concerns are very real.

However, Collegium is complicating things again with a substantial deal, one seeing a boost in net debt over a billion, even excluding the earnout. This and some ambiguity about the contribution of these new activities make it a bit difficult to get real conviction given the trends discussed above.

Collegium remains a fascinating case to study, and while shares have gradually moved up (albeit with some volatility), I am keen to keep a close eye on the business. While ambiguity remains the name of the game, I am intending to provide follow-up on this deal and the company at large.

data.title: Capital Southwest: Not A Big Enough Dip
data.description:
The private credit market has been hit hard, but Capital Southwest (CSWC) still trades near the highs. As with most other related stocks, the BDC, business development company, hasn't faced any real credit issues to warrant major stock weakness. My investment thesis is Neutral on the stock trading above NAV and far above the dip last October suggesting investors remain diligent and patient.

Strong History

Capital Southwest has operated as a BDC since 1988 and primarily finances transactions backed by private equity firms. The typical investment is amongst diverse industries and geographic regions in companies with an EBITDA between $3 and $25 million.

The typical loan leverage is 2.5x to 4.5x EBITDA with a loan-to-value of 25% to 50%. The loans are normally floating rate first liens, so Capital Southwest does face interest rate risk on any Fed rate cuts, though JPMorgan's chief U.S economist forecasts the Fed will not cut rates this year.

The BDC reported FQ3'26 total investment income of $61.4 million, with $4.6 million coming from PIK. The market is increasingly worried about such payment in kind income, where the company issues shares or adds interest to the debt balance versus paying cash interest as a sign of credit issues.

Capital Southwest obtains 93% of investment income from cash via interest and dividends, with 90% recurring. The stock has probably held value due to the limited PIK risk.

The company has only seen limited credit issues, with just 1.5% of the loan portfolio in non-accrual. Capital Southwest estimates roughly 90% of the $1.8 billion loan portfolio is performing as expected or better, with only $29.6 million of loans in the high-risk categories.

Due to the BDC classification, Capital Southwest has to return the majority of income to shareholders. The firm earned $0.60 of pre-tax net investment income during the quarter and paid out $0.64 in dividends.

Capital Southwest has now shifted to monthly dividends of $0.1934 with the Q2 amounts and dates already announced. The monthly dividend payouts likely help maintain the higher stock price with investors not trying to time the market, or having to wait 3 months for the next dividend.

The BDC now offers a 10.6% dividend yield on the regular monthly dividends. The firm also pays a quarterly supplemental dividend of $0.06 set to pay on June 30, which boosts the actual dividend yield to 11.6%.

As noted by the dividend yield chart, Capital Southwest regularly trades at similar or higher yields. The stock traded down to $17 back in late 2022 and early 2023 and the dividend yield surged.

No NAV Discount

The NAV boost in the quarter from $16.62 to $16.75 was almost entirely from accretion due to issuing equity above NAV. Capital Southwest sold $53 million shares during the quarter at an average price of $21.11 per share, about 27% above NAV.

The biggest risk is likely Fed rate cuts, though some analysts highlighted above are shifting towards not expecting rate cuts. Capital Southwest faces anywhere from under $4 million to over $11 million in annual NII impact from a change in base interest rates of 25 bps to 75 bps. On the flip side, the BDC would see a similar boost to NII on higher interest rates.

Of course, the ultimate risk in this sector is credit related. Capital Southwest only has ~1.5% of the $1.8 billion loan portfolio in non-accrual. The company estimates limited loans at risk while the weighted average yield of debt investments is up at 11.3%.

Based on available facts today, this BDC isn't a high risk investment. Regardless, the stock is likely to fall more and offer better entry points due to ongoing market fears in the space.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Capital Southwest offers a very strong dividend yield and no real reason to doubt the stability of the payouts. Considering the current environment, investors should just look for better entry points before being aggressive on a BDC trading this far above NAV despite the BDC offering an 11.6% dividend yield.

data.title: Reckitt Benckiser: Cheap Again Amid Macro Concerns
data.description:
Precisely what impact the Iran War will have on British consumer staples giant Reckitt Benckiser (RBGLY)(RBGPF) remains to be seen. One thing's for sure: weak consumer sentiment was already a pressing issue before the start of hostilities last month, and Reckitt is now facing the very real prospect that spiking energy prices will stoke inflation and maybe even lead to a global recession. At the very least, it's not going to make life any easier.

Unsurprisingly, this bleak assessment has weighed heavily on Reckitt's shares lately. The ADSs have actually done well since my last update in 2024, returning around 18%, but they were up as much as 40% just a few weeks ago. On the bright side, the stock is currently trading for less than 15x trailing adjusted EPS, so this does mean Reckitt has gotten pretty cheap again. Accordingly, I am leaving my "Buy" rating in place.

Demand Concerns

Reckitt owns well-known household and personal care brands like Dettol, Vanish, Durex, and Lysol. Via Mead Johnson, it also has a line in infant formula, though this is now a non-core unit that Reckitt is looking to exit. The core business accounted for just over 70% of Reckitt's revenue last year, 80% of which came from a collection of 11 power brands. These products typically command the number one or two positions in their respective categories.

These categories have relatively decent organic growth prospects. Consistent with this, revenue in Reckitt's core business rose 2.2% last year to £10.23 billion. This isn't as bad as it looks, as currency was a 280 basis point headwind last year. Like-for-like sales grew over 5%, with positive contributions from volumes (up 1.5%) and price/mix (up 3.7%).

Reckitt is a global business, and some places have been doing better than others. Emerging markets, which include India, China, Brazil, and more, have been good. Excluding its non-core units, Reckitt generated £4.29 billion in sales in these markets last year, mapping to like-for-like growth of roughly 15%. That performance was pretty balanced, too, with volumes (up 6.7%) and price/mix (up 7.9%) both contributing in healthy measures.

In contrast, Reckitt's more mature European markets have been quite weak. Reckitt's like-for-like revenue fell 1.4% in Europe last year, led by declining volumes (down over 3%). The short version is this: consumer sentiment is poor, and this is leading to down-trading into cheaper store-label brands. Europe kicked in £3.38 billion in revenue to Reckitt's core business last year. While not as much as the Emerging Markets segment, Europe's still one-third of the mix, and profit margins were higher there.

At this point, you probably don't need me to tell you what the bear case is. With energy prices exploding on the back of the Iran War, the issues I just mentioned don't look like they are about to go away. They could even get much worse, and this is just on the demand side of the equation.

Reckitt's 2025 earnings call took place about a week after the conflict began. When asked by one of the participating analysts what the impact was going to be, the CEO basically said he had no idea:

The range of possible outcomes, as you know, the uncertainty is extremely high. It's developing live, and we're just paying a lot of attention to what's going on, obviously, mostly focusing right now, as I said before, on the safety and well-being of our employees and of course, protecting our assets, our business. But it's too early for us to quantify any impacts.

That was two weeks ago. The situation hasn't exactly settled since then, so it's probably fair to assume that Reckitt is going to experience some kind of financial blowback.

Attractive Fundamentals

While that means there's a lot of uncertainty right now, Reckitt is still a solid business with attractive fundamentals. It will make a ton of money in any kind of "normal" environment. Last year is a good example. The company generated £3.54 billion in adjusted operating profit, for a margin of 24.9%. Free cash flow was £1.71 billion, and despite being negatively impacted by things like restructuring charges, this was still over 12% of revenue.

The thing I like most about Reckitt is the capital required to produce these profits is relatively modest. Between its level of inventories, receivables, and net property, plant & equipment, tangible assets only add up to around £6 billion. Whatever measure of earnings you want to use as the numerator, you can see that the return will be attractive. It's a very profitable enterprise.

The way I look at it, yes, the conflict in the Middle East may have some impact in the short run. But demand for detergents, disinfectants, and contraceptives isn't going to disappear. As for Reckitt, it spent 16.5% of its sales on marketing last year to keep its products front of mind. Not only that, but the potential for growth is pretty good, especially in less mature economies. Here's an interesting nugget from the company's 2025 results presentation: in the emerging markets in which Reckitt operates, households with over $25,000 in disposable income have increased by 60% these past ten years (adjusted for currency movements). There is a well of new customers to be tapped.

Given this, and accepting that I don't have a crystal ball, I think it's quite likely that Reckitt's brands will still be on the shelves in 5, 10, 15 years' time. That's the lens through which I am viewing the company.

The Valuation

Reckitt's ADSs trade for $13.62 as I type. A few weeks ago, they were over $17.50. The ADR to common share ratio is 5:1, and last year, the firm made £3.53 per share in adjusted net income. This points to a trailing price-to-earnings ratio of around 14.5. Similar companies like Procter & Gamble (PG), Clorox (CLX), and Church & Dwight (CHD) trade at sizeable premiums to this. Even Unilever (UL), which, like Reckitt, has its primary listing in London, commands a double-digit premium.

I don't think Reckitt deserves this kind of discount. We've already established that its underlying business is highly profitable, while its growth is okay once you account for currency movements. The discount isn't explained by leverage, either. Reckitt was sitting on £6.56 billion in net debt at the end of last year, equal to around 1.6 times adjusted EBITDA. That looks very reasonable for a global large-cap staples company.

Reckitt paid £2.12 per share in ordinary dividends against last year's earnings. The trailing yield is 4.2%. Adjusted for currency, core operating profit grew over 5%. It also spent £880 million on buybacks, reducing period-average shares outstanding by 3%. Between a 4%-plus starting yield, reasonable organic earnings growth, and some extra help from buybacks, it shouldn't be difficult for Reckitt to clear a 9-10% annualized hurdle in the long run. With the P/E ratio as low as it is, de-rating shouldn't be a headwind.

The "cost" is that there's a ton of near-term uncertainty right now, so some patience may be required. That isn't specific to Reckitt, though. And the company is cheaper than its peers, who will be facing similar problems. For a long-term-minded investor, this looks like an opportunistic entry point. I rate shares "Buy."

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: UP Fintech: The Market Still Misprices This Higher-Quality Fintech
data.description:
Investment Summary

UP Fintech (TIGR) is still valued like a Chinese, cyclical retail broker when in fact the business has become a higher-quality fintech platform, with less than 15% of clients' AUM now coming from Mainland China.

FY25 results confirmed that TIGR's stellar results in Q3/25 were not a one-off but a structural shift, with revenue up 56.3% YoY to $612.1 million and non-GAAP net income up 164.7% YoY to $186.5 million for the year. Q4/25 wasn't another sequential breakout quarter but confirmed that TIGR can sustain strong earnings from a much larger base, with monetization now going way beyond commissions. Other revenues were up 220.6% in Q4/25 to $30.8 million, and interest income remained very high at $71.3 million.

This matters as it indicates that TIGR is no longer a speculative, transaction-driven broker. The revenue base is increasingly supported by rising client asset quality and a diversification of monetization, supported by rising client balances, margin financing, securities lending, wealth products, Employee Stock Ownership Plan (ESOP) administration, IPO underwriting, and crypto-related initiatives, making the business more resilient and scalable moving forward.

FY25 and Q4/25 confirmed that balances, monetization, and platform economics are scaling, yet the current valuation does not reflect that shift. We believe this disconnect should close sooner rather than later.

Business Overview

TIGR is no longer a simple online broker but an Asia-focused mobile-first brokerage platform offering financial and wealth services, B2B services (ESOP services, IPO underwriting and distribution, institutional brokerage, execution services), market data, and community features.

TIGR's evolving position as a broker with additional financial services is a hybrid model that sits somewhere between Interactive Brokers (IBKR) and FUTU (FUTU). That said, TIGR is more intuitive and mobile-native than IBKR, less focused on China than FUTU, and less speculative and activity-driven than Robinhood (HOOD). TIGR is turning into a localized, mass-affluent financial hub, rather than a pure commission-based broker.

The business does indeed make money on commissions (40.3% of revenue in Q4/25), but also on interest income from margin financing and securities lending (40.6% of revenue in Q4/25), and on other income including wealth services, IPO underwriting, ESOP, market data, and crypto-related initiatives, which represented 17.5% of revenue in Q4.

This positioning allows TIGR to outperform with the cohort that generates the best economics due to higher quality balances: the Mass-Affluent Asian investors, the demographic between mass retail and high-net-worth individuals. This client cohort is particularly deep in Singapore (SG) and Hong Kong (HK), two of Asia's strongest wealth hubs. TIGR's Go-To-Market focuses on them and less so on low-value retail signups like most of its competitors (FUTU, Robinhood). TIGR further monetizes these premium investors through its financing, lending, and wealth services.

TIGR appears to attract premium clients, likely supported by the platform's mobile-first experience with access to the relevant markets for SG/HK investors (US, HK, Singapore), especially for young investors. Besides, its interface is intuitive without the complexity typically encountered with legacy brokers, yet upgraded options and margin trading features make it more relevant for sophisticated investors.

On top of that, in Singapore, TIGR appears to be one of the relatively few brokers that can be linked to CPF/SRS, two core pillars of the personal savings and retirement system in Singapore. This makes TIGR a one-stop platform for most, if not all, investment-related activities, different from the many trading apps present on the market. Such features make TIGR more embedded in local financial ecosystems and reinforce stickiness among its core Singaporean user base.

What The Market Thinks

The consensus narrative is that TIGR is indeed cheap but dependent on cyclically driven trading volume, exposed to Mainland China due to its activities, staff presence, and structure (the market tends to discount ADR and VIE Chinese setups due to regulatory risks), with slower account additions perceived as a sign that the business is decelerating as a whole.

While this concern is understandable, fear of TIGR being too exposed to Mainland China appears overblown, as less than 15% of the client AUM is based there, and virtually none of the new funded clients growth comes from there (discussed in more detail below).

Besides, the market is focusing on new account numbers (quantity) and less on the rising AUM per client and higher monetization per dollar of assets (quality). They also seem to discount the diversification of revenue, both in terms of product line (with non-trading activity rising meaningfully) and geographically. Recent market volatility, geopolitical tensions, and recession fears likely help explain the stock's inability to break out of its recent price range sustainably.

The Q4/25 earnings release seemingly did not fully change that perception, as the stock fell roughly 3% after the release. The market saw profits retreating slightly after an exceptional Q3, while commission revenue remained flat QoQ despite total notional volume rising roughly 50%. That could indeed raise a few eyebrows, but it is less alarming than it may seem at first glance.

All this noise is what may create an opportunity: the stock price is down almost 50% from last year's highs in September, just as the new strategy produces a structurally more robust, less volatile, and likely higher earning baseline moving forward, as shown by the numbers.

What The Numbers Actually Show

On the positive side, FY25 revenue went up 56.3% to $612.1 million, with non-GAAP net income reaching $186.5 million, up 164.7%. Q4/25 revenue reached $175.6 million, up 41.5% YoY, with non-GAAP net income reaching $48.9 million, up 60.5% YoY. Total client AUM on the platform reached $60.8 billion, a 45.7% YoY increase, with funded accounts going up +14.8% YoY to 1.254 million.

Options and futures contracts rose from 25.6 million to 26.8 million QoQ, and trading volume grew from $209.4 billion to $316.6 billion QoQ. In Q4, operating leverage is also showing through a 12.7% YoY reduction in execution and clearing expenses thanks to self-clearing of US/HK equities, despite much higher activity.

Trading activity surged from $209.4 billion in Q3/25 to $316.6 billion in Q4/25, which is up more than 50% QoQ, a huge jump, yet commission revenue was flat QoQ. The thing is, stock trading volume, a more relevant indicator for TIGR than total notional volume, went from $73.4 billion to $79.6 billion QoQ (up 8.4%), while the average cash-equity take rate declined from 7.1 bps to 6.4 bps. Management said it is due to fewer mini stocks being traded in Q4 than in Q3. This suggests that the disconnect between rapidly growing total notional volume and a flat commission revenue line reflects a temporary shift in activity composition rather than a demand slowdown.

Other revenues stood at $30.8 million (up 220.6%), above Q3's $26.3 million, mostly driven by wealth management services. Management added on the earnings call that roughly 20% of new users in licensed markets use wealth management services, mainly in SG and HK. Structured-note volume rose more than 50% QoQ, while mutual-fund balances almost doubled YoY. This strongly suggests that TIGR is monetizing idle capital beyond cash balances.

The same applies to TIGR's B2B ecosystem: in Q4, the business underwrote 22 IPOs for a total of 47 in FY25. In HK, the total IPO subscription amount doubled QoQ in the quarter, a significant improvement. The ESOP business added 39 new clients, as per management, reaching a total of 748 at the end of FY25. This shows that TIGR is also generating meaningful non-retail fee income on top of its core brokerage operations.

What is more nuanced about Q4 is that revenue was basically flat QoQ (with commissions and interest income slightly down QoQ) despite higher volume. Q4 non-GAAP earnings were down sequentially from Q3. The slight margin dip mainly came from an additional $4 million spent in marketing and around $2.6 million in communication, crypto data AI, and cloud-related costs. That said, these expenses were growth-oriented and could provide a payback in the coming quarters. SG&A was also impacted by a $3 million bad debt provision related to an IPO underwriting that was recognized last year but not yet paid, but this was just a one-off adjustment.

Raw client additions also continued to slow down. The funded account additions fell from 60K in Q1/25 to 31.5K in Q3, now standing at 29.7K in Q4. That said, the business surpassed its 150K net new funded clients in FY25 by reaching the 161.9K figure, and FY25 net inflows went over $10 billion, with Q4 net inflows alone reaching $3 billion. This suggests that overall client funding stayed robust despite the slight deceleration in new account growth.

This is supported by the fact that TIGR's premium client base keeps funding their accounts in significant ways. In Q4/25, client assets in Singapore rose more than 50% YoY, with an average net inflow per newly acquired client at $62,000. In HK, where TIGR recorded a record-high average net asset inflow per newly acquired client of $43,000 ($30,000 the prior quarter), client assets more than tripled, with payback periods among the shortest across its licensed markets. This further supports the idea that focusing on premium client acquisition makes sense for TIGR.

In Australia and New Zealand, client assets more than doubled. This shows that TIGR has a lot of traction with premium users, who seem to fund their accounts more aggressively over time. Indeed, in Q4/25, management said that roughly 35% of new funded clients came from SG, 35% from HK, 25% from ANZ, and around 5% from the US, showing that international markets are the main focus of TIGR, not Mainland China.

TIGR has a dual monetization model combining balance-driven and activity-driven revenue streams, shaping how TIGR monetizes in different market environments. TIGR makes money in up-trending markets when its balance sheet expands, as clients portfolios increase in value, net inflows accelerate, and margin usage increases. This means higher interest income, more assets in wealth products, and larger cash balances that TIGR can monetize. During volatile and choppy markets, TIGR capitalizes primarily on trading activity spikes, options usage rises, and retail engagement strengthens. This means more commissions, more derivatives activity, and typically higher engagement.

Bear markets are, of course, the weak spot for digital brokers. Trading activity may not decline too fast initially, as there are activity spikes during panic phases, but engagement can eventually drop. Net inflows may slow or reverse, and portfolios fall in value. It is also harder to get new clients, which could put pressure on customer acquisition costs (CAC). TIGR is therefore not immune to a prolonged bear market, but its diversification makes it more resilient than transaction-driven brokers.

All in all, Q4/25 was not a perfect quarter, but it reinforced our view that the broader economic engine behind the business is actually reinforcing as monetization keeps expanding and as TIGR diversifies its revenues to rely less on raw trading volume. Interestingly, Q3 and Q4 had two very different quarterly profiles: in Q3 commission revenue remained high despite lower trading volume, whereas in Q4 trading activity resumed very high with modest commission revenue growth, yet other monetization lines kept scaling. This further suggests that TIGR no longer depends on a single market regime.

The Decisive Variable

The strategic shift is that TIGR no longer optimizes for raw user count but for asset growth and monetization depth through the acquisition of mass-affluent or high-net-worth clients. Their dual monetization model is directly amplified by one decisive variable: the inflow of premium clients. High-quality users drive large net inflows: indeed, net inflows reached $3 billion in a single quarter (Q4), which represents roughly 5% of the entire client AUM.

These inflows in turn translate into bigger cash balances that drive interest income, margin financing, and securities lending while also supporting higher-value financial offerings such as trading options, futures, etc. This is what is ultimately making earnings structurally higher. The fact that TIGR's costs are largely fixed means that this virtuous cycle ends up falling disproportionately to the bottom line.

If this positive feedback loop keeps working, then earnings will rise structurally even with normalizing volume. This will in part depend on market conditions and execution discipline, especially when it comes to CAC and operating leverage, as TIGR keeps investing in future growth. Otherwise, TIGR will remain a less promising broker evolving in a competitive space.

What would weaken the thesis is a slowdown in premium client inflows, a stagnation or reduction in client AUM, or a deterioration of client cash balances. So far this has not been the case; strong inflows continued, and margins are holding up despite aggressive investments made in marketing for future growth. This suggests that TIGR's expansion is more structural than cyclical.

Valuation

In FY25, TIGR generated $186.5 million of non-GAAP net income for a company trading at a $1.25 billion market cap. TIGR therefore trades at 6.7x P/E TTM, which seems cheap given that they grow at a double-digit rate with a net cash position of over $400 million. Despite the relative cyclicality typically associated with financial brokers, the durability of TIGR's earnings (driven by improving balances and monetization rather than one-off volume spikes) appears more robust than the current valuation suggests.

Company Funded Clients Q4/25 Client Assets USD Q4/25 Market Cap USD Market Cap / Client Market Cap / Client Assets
TIGR 1.25M ~$60.8B $1.25B ~$1K ~2.1%
FUTU 3.36M ~$157B $20.14B ~$6K ~12.8%
HOOD 27.0M ~$324B $67.43B ~$2.5k ~20.8%
IBKR 4.40M* ~$779.9B $116.12B ~$26.3k ~14.9%

*HOOD reports the numbers of "accounts" which is a proxy for funded clients, although not strictly funded accounts, which may slightly overstate this figure.

TIGR is trading at roughly ~6x to 10x cheaper than peers on an AUM basis. It is also extremely cheap on a per-client basis. FUTU is the closest peer, yet it trades at 6x higher value per client, despite TIGR's faster international growth in the SG, HK, and ANZ markets. TIGR's valuation at 2.1% of client assets (against 13% to 21% for larger peers) is very cheap, given its strong asset growth and profitability.

This valuation disconnect seems even more apparent projecting estimated EPS in FY26 and FY27:

Metrics (TIGR) Q4/25 FY25 FY26E FY27E
Revenue $175.6M $612.1M $780M $950M
Revenue growth YoY +41.5% +56.3% +27% +22%
Net margin 25.7% 27.9% 27.0% 26.5%
Net income $45.2M $170.9M $211M $252M
Diluted Shares ~188M ~185M ~185M ~185M
EPS $0.24 $0.93 $1.14 $1.36

This table assumes a slight deceleration in net margins over time (conservative). Indeed, even with scale progressively diluting fixed costs and higher AUM generating more interest income, interest rates are likely to go down in the coming quarters, which should slightly compress net interest income. Besides, TIGR is ramping up marketing expenses quite aggressively as they expand in various international markets. Competition could also add pricing pressure over time on take rates. We also anticipate a slight normalization of growth, as FY25 was particularly aggressive in that regard, with price appreciation and volatility across markets in Q2/Q3 of that year.

Even taking into account margin and growth normalization, TIGR's EPS could still reach roughly $1.36 by the end of FY27 (18 months away), meaning at today's price the stock has an 18-month forward valuation of 5x EPS, or 6x FWD EPS taking FY26E into account. This is very cheap compared to peers with similar growth profiles.

Even applying an ADR/VIE-related discount, a 10x P/E multiple does not seem far-fetched. This would imply a rough $11.5 price per share. A 12x multiple (still lower than FUTU's P/E TTM multiple, the second cheapest of peers by some margin) would imply a rough $13.5 price target, close to 100% higher than where the stock trades at today.

This estimate falls broadly in line with the target that most analysts covering the stock have, as seen below:

We think the valuation gap could close fairly soon, helped by institutions that have recently been accumulating the stock, as per the graph below:

Conclusion

Q4/25 and FY25 reinforced our bullish stance on UP Fintech. These results confirmed that the business is becoming more international and diversified than the market narrative implies.

The decisive variable of the thesis was never notional trading volume but whether premium clients' assets would continue compounding and whether TIGR could expand its monetization across various product offerings. Financial results confirmed that view. Net inflows remained robust. Account balances stayed elevated. Wealth and B2B services grew rapidly, and operating leverage remained visible despite a temporary compression in margins (QoQ) in Q4 explained by aggressive growth investments.

We believe that some discount related to the ADR/VIE structure and the relative cyclicality of the industry (a bear market could impact the financials as detailed above) could justify a small discount, but the current valuation seems way too disconnected from peers and fundamentals, suggesting that a rerating remains the most likely scenario in our view. We believe that the current fair value sits somewhere between $11.5 and $13.5, with a midpoint of $12.5, roughly 80-85% higher than today's price.

data.title: Silver Price Steadies At $70 While Bond Yields Stay Firm After Fed Hold
data.description:
By Ezequiel Gomes

​Silver (XAGUSD:CUR) traded on a calmer footing on Friday, March 20, with spot prices hovering at $70 after the previous session’s sharp unwind knocked the market to its lowest levels in weeks. The bounce was modest rather than decisive. Traders were still contending with a Federal Reserve that left rates unchanged at 3.50-3.75%, a U.S. 10-year yield holding above 4.27%, and Brent crude that remained elevated near $108-110 even after retreating from a spike above $119.

The chart is trying to build a floor, but it has not earned one yet. After Thursday’s collapse toward the mid-$60 zone, silver spent Friday recovering only part of that damage, which leaves the market looking more like it is pausing than reversing. A close back above $71 would help steady the tone, but that alone would not erase the weakness left by the week’s breakdown.

What matters immediately is the band between $71 and $73. That zone now carries the weight of failed support turned overhead supply, and traders will want to see price reclaim it with more conviction before talking about a cleaner rebound. If that happens, the next area in view is around $75-76, where selling accelerated earlier in the week.

On the downside, the market still has unfinished business around Thursday’s low. The $65 region is the key fault line for short-term sentiment, because another break there would suggest the liquidation phase is not done and that buyers are still too tentative to absorb macro pressure. Until silver can stop producing violent intraday swings, momentum remains fragile.

Rates are doing the talking again

The biggest anchor remains monetary policy. The Fed kept the target range for the federal funds rate at 3.50-3.75%, and updated projections continued to signal a cautious path for easing, leaving precious metals with little relief from the rates side of the story. That matters for silver because the metal tends to struggle when real return alternatives start looking more attractive again.

Treasury yields reinforced that message through Friday trading. Even with some intraday back and forth, the U.S. 10-year yield stayed around 4.27-4.37%, while shorter-dated yields also held firm enough to keep financing conditions feeling tight. In practical terms, the bond market has stopped giving metals the benefit of the doubt.

Energy remains the wild card. Fresh attacks on Gulf infrastructure kept oil markets unsettled, and although Brent pulled back from Thursday’s intraday surge above $119, it was still trading near $108-110 by Friday. That combination of conflict risk and expensive crude is feeding inflation nerves rather than classic safe haven behavior, which helps explain why silver has not responded to geopolitical stress in the way some traders might normally expect.

What comes next may depend on whether volatility cools

There is a credible recovery path from here, but it needs cooperation from the macro backdrop. If oil settles further, yields stop climbing, and silver can regain the low $70s with less erratic price action, the market could work its way back toward $73 first and then test the mid-$70 zone. That would look less like a full trend change than a repair phase after forced selling.

The opposite case is still easy to picture. Another jump in crude, another push higher in yields, or renewed fund liquidation would leave silver exposed to a return toward $65. For now, the metal is trading like a market that needs calmer inflation signals before it can rebuild confidence.

Silver has given back a large portion of the explosive gains that defined its earlier 2026 run, and that reset is forcing traders to decide whether the move was overheated or merely interrupted. The answer matters beyond one metal, because silver often sits at the intersection of inflation expectations, industrial demand, and broader risk appetite.

This material may contain third-party opinions; none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.

Original Post

data.title: Richemont: The Market Has Likely Front-Run Its Risks, An Entry Point Has Emerged
data.description:
Luxury retail stocks have struggled since the turn of the year amid softening of the consumer cycle and signs of growing risk-aversion. Despite the myriad of cyclical headwinds, I think Compagnie Financière Richemont SA (CFRHF) has presented an opportunity on the basis of its through-the-cycle growth and a recent overreaction from investors.

Herewith is an explanation of my claims.

Business Overview

Richemont is a luxury retailer that sells Veblen goods. For those unaware, Veblen is just a label used for a type of good that targets ultra-wealthy clients. The thought process is: The ultra-wealthy won't spend cyclically; they'll buy throughout-the-cycle, contingent on your product satisfying their demands.

A broad-based view of Richemont's business model illustrates three dominant segments, namely Jewelry, Watches, and Other; here's a short breakdown of each.

Jewelry: As per its latest fiscal year, this segment spanned ~73% of revenue. The business unit includes renowned brands such as Cartier, Van Cleef & Arpels, Vhernier, and Buccellati. Echoed by its 99% contribution to operating profits during the year, the jewelry segment has seemingly established itself as a leader with significant pricing power.

Watchmakers: Richemont's watchmakers segment comprised around 15% of group revenue in its latest fiscal year. However, profit margins seem more sensitive as the segment contributed to ~1.96% of operating profits, a 69% year-over-year decrease. Notable brands include IWC, Piaget, Panerai, Vacheron Constantin, Jaeger-LeCoultre, and A. Lange & Söhne.

Others: This segment is arguably Richment's most cyclical. It suffered a common-size operating result loss of roughly 1.65% during the group's latest fiscal year. The unit spans ~1.23% of group revenue and largely comprises of fashion items as well as pipeline retailers such as Watchfinder & Co.; among its most prominent fashion brands are dunhill, Montblanc, Chloé, and Peter Millar.

Side Note: Richemont's fashion and others segments are intertwined in certain reporting items and distinct in others. I assumed others and fashion as singular.

A geographic view of the business' operations illustrates a dependence on Europe and North America, which collectively spanned roughly 48% of sales in Richemont's latest reporting period. However, the group has shown increasing representation in Asia Pacific, the Middle East, and Japan recently. Development into Asia and the Middle East seems suitable, given the consolidation of ultra-wealthy clients.

Richemont still sells 70% of its products directly in-store and 6% of its products through online retail. The company generates approximately 24% of its revenue through wholesale and royalty income. Although some may disagree, I think Richemont's in-store presence is an advantage, given the fact that it sells luxury items - the shopping experience matters, and location matters.

Richemont's Recent Market Performance

Richemont's stock and American Depository Receipts have performed poorly in recent quarters, which I mostly attribute to a systematic sell-off in luxury items. My claim is substantiated by the contemporaneous sell-off in the KraneShares Global Luxury Index ETF (KLXY) and a close Richemont peer, namely LVMH Moet Hennessy Louis Vuitton SE (LVMHF). Why did the systematic sell-off occur? To my best interpretation, the sell-off occurred due to a combination of risk-off selling after the U.S.-Iran war as well as a deteriorating cyclical consumer outlook on Chinese spending, which has left luxury retailers such as Richemont vulnerable.

I see an opportunity to buy low after Richemont's recent decline. As illustrated by the following figure, the stock is known for long-term growth with occasional cyclical declines. In addition, Richemont's latest decline has opened up a forward dividend yield of approximately 2.23%, which is arguably a solid carry component for a high quality-growth stock.

In a nutshell, consumer cyclicality and risk-sentiment have dominated during recent months. As a result, we've seen Richemont's risk premium open up – I won't mind investing before the risk cycle moderates while being cognizant of the stock's max drawdown risk.

Fundamental Reasons for The Stock's Recent Decline

I've mostly mentioned Richemont's business model and recent stock performance up until now. I've also claimed that I think it's a buy-the-dip opportunity. However, the question beckons: Does Richemont have long-term fundamental attributes that would substantiate a recovery? My answer is yes; here's why.

As illustrated by figure 6, Richemont has immaculate 5-year average profitability margins. For example, it sports a return-on-equity of 15.79%, which is comfortably above both its December year-end U.S. and Swiss cost of equity capital (measured by the CAPM method).

  • U.S.-Based CAPM = Risk-free rate + (Market Risk Premium)*American Depository Receipt Beta = 4.18% + (2.37%)*1.07 = 6.72%
  • Swiss-Based CAPM = Risk-free rate + (Market Risk Premium)*Equity Beta = 0.30% + (5.33%)*1.07 = 6%

Furthermore, the company has a 5-year average gross margin of 65.18%, which echoes economies of scale, and a 5-year average EBITDA margin, which reflects income statement efficiency. In addition, Richemont has been able to achieve these figures without surrendering growth – the company has a ten-year compound annual growth rate of 7%, which I deem compelling as it endured periods such as the COVID-19 pandemic and 2022's economic turbulence. A look at the 5-year growth rate shows a CAGR of 12.24%.

A point-in-time observation: A closer look at Richemont's Q1 sales report (figure 7) shows similar parallels to the Group's throughout-the-cycle results. As previously mentioned, Richemont recently experienced a decline in watchmaker revenue, which is to be expected - watch prices are cyclical. However, jewelry sold strongly in Q1, gaining by 7% year-over-year, which lifted Richemont's Group sales growth to 3% year-over-year, on a nominal basis. In terms of geography (figure 7), Richemont recorded most of its Q1 growth from Europe and the Americas amid a continuation in the easing of monetary cycles and stability from jewelry sales. As previously mentioned, the Group struggled with Asia Pacific amid sluggish consumer sentiment, conveyed by a 4% year-over-year decrease in sales. An important side-note would relate to FX rates, Richemont's constant-FX sales grew by 6%, suggesting the company faced FX translation headwinds amid the strengthening of the Euro - I don't expect Euro strengthening to be a permanent feature. In addition, Japan's sales dropped by 13% year-over-year on an FX-adjusted basis – Japan remains a small part of Group sales. However, I would keep an eye on its developments due to evolving signs of fiscal dominance, which could disrupt both FX and consumer behavior.

Although it most recently released its H1 sales report, Richemont has yet to deliver its comprehensive results for calendar 2026. Rolling back to calendar year 2025 (Figure 8), we can see solid progression in operating margins, despite headwinds from watchmaker sales and inconsistent Asia Pacific demand. The company's operating margin is stable at 22.2% and it has a fortress liquidity base, shown by its € 6.52 billion net cash position. Per-share profitability climbed to € 3.078; however, it should be noted that Richemont had suffered an impact from discontinued operations in 2024, which lowered the group's profitability on a non-core basis.

A closer look at Richemont's balance sheet shows stability. The group's asset-liability ratio is ~2.16 and its goodwill and other intangible assets comprise merely 12% of its asset base, reflecting a high distance-to-default and potentially low asset volatility. Moreover, Richemont has a €733 billion net tax asset (author's calculations) and provisions of merely €239 million. In essence, we are looking at a low-risk, high-quality balance sheet with plenty of growth potential.

Does Richemont have growth potential? I would say so. The numbers suggest its brands are growing organically and would be hard to dethrone, given the high-barriers-to-entry in the ultra luxury goods environment. Furthermore, Richemont has the necessary liquidity to acquire externally either through its holding company or through its subsidiaries. That being said, Richemont's venture into Asia raises challenges, as expansion would need to be perfectly geared toward localized demand.

Multiples

Multiples Are Cyclically & Relatively Underpriced

Figure 10 points to Richemont ADR's multiples, which are quite elevated in absolute terms. However, high multiples are a salient feature of high quality companies with growth attributes. For example, LVMH has a P/E ratio of 20.93x, which is slightly below Richemont's, but it should be noted that LVMH has faced a year-over-year growth contraction (-4.58%). On a cyclical basis, Richemont's salient multiples are at a slight discount to their normalized averages. For instance, the ADR's Enterprise Value/Sales ratio is at a 13.48% discount, its price-to-cash flow ratio at a 9.98% discount and its Enterprise Value/EBIT at a 17.63% discount – I think Richemont multiples are commendable at this stage, it remains highly unlikely that its multiples will enter deep value territory, simply due to the company's high-quality and growth-orientated business model.

I don't really see Richemont as a bet on multiples. As previously mentioned, I think we are looking at a case of a technically exaggerated sell-off, which is somewhat substantiated by the ADR's RSI pulling down to the 30 handle. I think the sell-off is unjustified as Richemont's fundamentals have shown to perform throughout cycles, including 2022, where we saw a similar macro environment (inflation shock, high oil prices, richly priced stock market).

Risk Factors

High Max Drawdown

I previously highlighted Richemont's noteworthy max drawdown, which I'd like to highlight again as my argument essentially centers around buying the dip. The stock's substantial max drawdown warrants close attention, as it suggests investors might have to average down into weakness for a considerable time before reversion occurs. In fact, mean-reversion isn't guaranteed, especially for single issuers.

Asia Pacific Overhang

Richemont's endeavor into Asia seems trivial. However, it's a different market from what it's used to. I fear that a lack of local consumer understanding and sustained pressure on the Chinese consumer might result in an overhang if not managed carefully.

Final Word

I'm adding Richemont to Pearl Gray's watchlist as I believe the company's stock has priced risks preemptively. Although higher geopolitical risk and softening of the consumer cycle somewhat justify Richemont's recent performance, I'm banking on a recovery – I think this is an excellent stock to average down into.

I'm currently in the process of re-arranging our portfolio's risk-return metrics. I'm keeping Richemont on my watchlist and might add it as soon as our cash position has solidified.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Dow Jones And U.S. Stock Market Outlook - U.S. Yields Explode, Dampening Investor Sentiment
data.description:
By Elior Manier

Today is providing yet another leg of renewed inflation fears, and this is not boding well for global assets.

Yesterday's address by Israeli PM Netanyahu raised hopes for a shorter-than-expected conflict, leading to major dumping in the US dollar and WTI oil. Still, this narrative quickly took a significant U-turn.

President Trump's latest pledge to militarily intervene in the Strait of Hormuz came with a surprise: a repricing of a now much longer US-Iran conflict to achieve more optimistic results.

The new idea arises from the US Army's plan to capture Kharg Island, Iran's largest energy production site, to pressure the IRGC into reopening the Strait of Hormuz.

The issue, however, is the fact that this would require boots on the ground, and sources familiar with the issue pointed to at least a month extra of strikes to weaken the Islamic regime further.

As Fed's Waller pointed out in a first post-FOMC interview, the issue is that a longer conflict poses the risk to disanchor inflation expectations, and this is what got the rates market to blow up this morning. Any hopes for rate cuts are now long gone.

US yields are up significantly in this morning's action, implying higher inflation ahead and even potential rate hikes: The US 2-year yield (US2Y) is now at 3.90% (while the current Fed Funds is at 3.75%).

The issue here is that stock markets, particularly when the economy begins to decelerate, can be largely disrupted by higher yields and costs.

But inflation may have to come first in the event of a longer conflict, to avoid not only job losses but also further erosion of the US dollar's buying power.

Bond vigilantes, not liking the idea of a longer conflict, and selling Treasuries, indicate this discontent. The repricing to higher rates will weigh on risk assets for the time being.

At least, the US dollar (DXY) used this to recover most of its past session's move. What a fakeout!

With oil prices and the US dollar decoupling since the FOMC meeting, both will now need to be tracked individually to spot where the market wants to go.

The 4H 50-period MA on the Dollar Index will serve as a major indicator of upcoming action.

Extending above it would hurt sentiment even further. Rejecting it, however, may soothe the selloff in stock markets a bit more. But don't expect to see much clarity before the end of the weekend.

Let's spot how this translates into stock markets by diving into today’s mid-session charts and trading levels for the major US indexes: the Dow Jones (DJI), Nasdaq (NDX), and S&P 500 (SP500).

Current Session's Stock Heatmap

Once again, the Energy sector is the only survivor of this daily bloodshed, with Tech and Utilities being the largest victims of the explosion in yields.

Dow Jones 4H Chart and Trading Levels

The DJIA is back at its previous session lows, right in the key 45,700 August 2025 support.

Bouncing from here will be mandatory to hope for a more concise rebound, because the 50,000 level now looks far gone.

Today will probably not be the day to see a breakout either to the upside or downside, when considering the huge doji candle. As long as yields remain higher, the downside does take the upper hand.

For an upside breakout, however, investors will want to see a break and 1H close above 46,012.

Dow Jones Technical Levels for Trading:

Resistance Levels

  • 46,012 Doji highs (short-term bullish above)
  • March 8 war lows pivot 46,300
  • Resistance 47,000 +/- 100 points (channel top)
  • Pivotal Resistance 47,500-47,650
  • Key Resistance at 48,000
  • 48,400-48,500 mini-resistance

Support Levels

  • 45,700-45,900 August support and channel lows) - bearish below
  • August highs 45,715
  • 45,000 psychological level (main support on higher time frame)

Nasdaq 4H Chart and Trading Levels

Nasdaq is now breaking its solid range, particularly hurt by the higher yields. Bulls did make an attempt to return back around the 24,150 support zone, but bears are taking the upper hand.

Except for much better sentiment after the weekend, expect to see a test of the 23,800 support zone.

Nasdaq Technical Levels of Interest:

Resistance Levels

  • 24,300 session highs, range holds above (low odds)
  • 24,450-25,550 range pivot (short-term resistance)
  • Mini-intraday resistance 24,750
  • Key resistance 25,000-25,200 (range highs - long-term bullish above)

Support Levels

  • February support 24,150-24,200 (testing, bearish below)
  • October-November support 23,800-24,000
  • Early 2025 ATH at 22,000-22,229 support

S&P 500 1H Chart and Trading Levels

The S&P 500 broke its previous session's double bottom, but dip buyers have not said their last word.

The outlook looks fragile, but rebounding here will be required for a return to 6,700, as the S&P 500 is now evolving within a bear channel.

Look at today's highs (6,600) and lows (6,540) - above will hold the bull channel, below will mark a further correction back towards 6,500.

S&P 500 technical levels of interest:

Resistance Levels

  • Session highs 6,600
  • Momentum pivot 6,640
  • 6,680-6,700 mini-resistance
  • 6,740 key intraday resistance
  • Pivotal resistance 6,770-6,800

Support Levels

  • Session lows (6,540)
  • 6,490-6,512 October lows immediate support
  • 6,400 major psychological support

Safe trades, and keep track of WTI prices, yields, and the US dollar!

Original Post

data.title: ETB: Broader Market Volatility Brings Some Discount Widening
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

The Eaton Vance Tax-Managed Buy-Write Income Fund (ETB) is a closed-end fund that provides investors with exposure to an S&P 500 Index-like portfolio and applies a call writing strategy. These short calls are against the S&P 500 Index itself and can provide some option premiums to help fuel the monthly distribution, along with capital gains from the underlying portfolio. Primarily, in a rising market, the distribution will be covered through the underlying capital gains in the portfolio rather than the actual option premiums.

Since our last update, the fund has provided solid total returns. It has nearly matched the S&P 500 Index itself, despite being a call-writing fund. Which most investors know, the call writing strategy can lead to underperformance. This occurs primarily in a sharply rising market, which we actually saw. Even further, the fund's discount has widened a touch since our last update—meaning the actual underlying total returns from the portfolio would have exceeded its total share price return.

ETB Basics

  • 1-Year Z-score: -1.00
  • Discount/Premium: -7.22%
  • Distribution Yield: 8.59%
  • Expense Ratio: 1.11%
  • Leverage: N/A.
  • Managed Assets: $480.71 million
  • Structure: Perpetual

ETB's investment objective is “to provide current income and gains, with a secondary objective of capital appreciation.”

To achieve that objective, they'll focus on

a diversified portfolio of common stocks and write call options on one or more U.S. indices on a substantial portion of the value of its common stock portfolio to seek to generate current earnings from the option premium.

Performance Comparison

ETB targets a near 100% overwrite of their notional value. Given that, it can limit the upside potential when the market rises sharply, as the upside can be capped. So this is something to be aware of, and is one of the cons of call writing funds—though the distributions are certainly attractive.

The good news is that with the underlying portfolio mirroring the index, the underlying holdings can still continue to appreciate. This acts as a sort of strategy to provide a "covered" call scenario even though they are writing index options, which can't be owned directly.

Additionally, the fund rolls these trades every couple of days, usually at roughly two weeks out, using a laddered approach. The shorter duration means they can adjust the trades more regularly, selecting various strike prices. While the trades below have expired at this point from the annual report, it is worth noting as an example of how the fund is positioned at any given time.

For some comparisons with some of the popular ETF peers, we can look at the Global X S&P 500 Covered Call ETF (XYLD) and NEOS S&P 500 High Income ETF (SPYI). SPYI is a newer fund, so the performance track record doesn't go back as far.

SPYI utilizes a laddered call writing strategy but rolls its options out about every month, with a flexible overwrite level. XYLD also writes their options about a month out, but targets a near 100% overwrite like ETB.

In terms of the total NAV returns, ETB and SPYI have mirrored each other quite closely. XYLD, on the other hand, has done quite poorly on that front.

Now, of course, we don't want to ignore the total share price returns. After all, that is actually the performance that investors had given you, trade shares and not NAV. Looking back at this period highlights the specific opportunities (or downfalls) that can come with investing in a CEF structure. That is the wildly varying discounts/premiums that can occur. In this case, ETB had been trading at a premium before that premium collapsed into a deep discount.

Today, that isn't the case, and that's why it makes it a more tempting opportunity. Looking back historically, the fund had generally traded at a premium until around 2022. Given the broader market volatility, the discount widening out a touch here makes sense. Additionally, given the broader equity declines, it also makes it a more tempting time to start adding to some positions.

I think that it is primarily related to the distribution cut they had done. Since CEFs often tend to deliver level distributions over time, when they do get adjusted, they often get punished. Since most ETFs pay variable distributions each quarter or month, that doesn't tend to be the case for them—and besides, ETFs only trade at negligible discount/premium levels as well.

This also highlights another reason for the potential discount, with the rise of ETF competition. Investors can invest in something like SPYI and not deal with the discount/premium. As we saw, it delivered similar results anyway, even technically outperforming a touch. So for investors that don't want to deal with discounts/premiums and trading around those occasionally, an ETF can be the better structure.

Steady Monthly Distribution

While the fund cut its distribution in late 2022, it had actually raised it again a couple of years later. Nearly to the same level as it is currently $0.1058 and had been $0.1080. In general, ETB has not made many adjustments at all since its inception.

For some income investors, the managed distribution policies provide a level amount for lengthy periods of time. That could be one bonus relative to ETFs for those types of investors.

However, just because the fund hasn't historically made many adjustments in its payout does not mean that it couldn't in the future. It depends entirely on the overall market. We happened to have a very strong period of equity performance during most of this fund's life.

The fund writes options that can generate option premiums to help fund the distribution. However, most of the heavy lifting is being funded by the actual underlying portfolio's capital gains in a rising market. In fact, most of the time, the options written generate capital losses in a rising market. This is because those trades get closed out at losses when the S&P 500 Index rises above the strike prices selected. For 2025, we can see that the annual report reflects around $12.5 million in realized losses from writing options.

On the flip side, in a flat or downward market, the option premiums received can generate realized gains—which then can be used to help support some of the distribution being paid out. That kind of balance is why the fund can often generate realized losses in any market conditions, rising or falling. This is one of the ways that it is "tax-managed," as its name would suggest. The realized losses can generate distributions classified as return of capital, which is a way to defer tax obligations because it reduces an investor's cost basis.

Additionally, there are significant classifications of long-term capital gains as well. That is also tax-friendly because it is a lower relative tax rate when compared to ordinary income. Finally, the small amount of ordinary income that is often generated often qualifies for qualified dividend treatment. For example, in 2025, 100% of the ordinary income was considered qualified.

ETB's Portfolio

For the most part, we already discussed the actual active part of the fund's strategy through its writing index calls. When it comes to the portfolio, it is pretty boring, as it doesn't change often and simply is a reflection of the S&P 500 Index. That includes its heavier emphasis on the technology sector, but it does so with around 140 holdings instead of the ~500.

Given the sector weighting and those who might be familiar with the S&P 500 Index, we'll also see the top holdings dominated by the Magnificent 7. Of course, with the smaller number of holdings, the weighting of the positions that are left will be higher.

At this time, the top ten account for 36.35% of the SPDR S&P 500 ETF (SPY). It reports daily, so there is some time delay between the top ten and ETB's below, but it is still worth a comparison. For ETB, the top ten accounted for 42.71%.

Conclusion

ETB has seen its discount widen out a touch since our prior update. Along with the broader market entering into pullback territory, it has made the fund a more interesting prospect recently. However, I'd still like to see the discount get even wider before wanting to buy more aggressively. Alternatively, using a dollar-cost average approach could be an appealing choice as well. That would be buying small batches of shares here and there, starting to receive some distributions, but also leaving the door open to add at lower levels should we get into a correction or bear territory.
